findings.title: Soft-delete tombstones remain visible through active retrieval and listing surfaces
findings.summary: When SPECKIT_SOFT_DELETE_TOMBSTONES is enabled and the schema has deleted_at, memory_delete and memory_bulk_delete update only memory_index.deleted_at. They do not remove active_memory_projection rows, vector payloads, memory_fts rows, BM25 documents, or caches. Multiple active read paths then omit m.deleted_at IS NULL and can keep returning tombstoned memories.

evidence.detail: The feature-gated tombstone path runs UPDATE memory_index SET deleted_at and returns without calling vectorIndex.deleteMemory.
Bulk delete uses the same tombstone helper inside the transaction.
The hard-delete path removes vector payloads, ancillary rows, embedding cache entries, memory_index row, search caches, and BM25 documents; tombstoning bypasses this cleanup.
Vector search, multi-concept search, folder listing, and usage stats join or read active rows without a deleted_at predicate.
FTS5 search joins memory_fts to memory_index without filtering m.deleted_at IS NULL.
Trigger and structural retrieval build active result sets without filtering deleted tombstones.
Graph/causal FTS and LIKE seed paths also lack deleted_at filters.

findings.impact: A caller can delete a memory, receive successful deletion semantics, and still have the record appear in search, trigger, graph, folder, or stats surfaces. This is a retention/privacy correctness failure for the soft-delete mode and can also poison downstream ranking through stale projections and vectors.
findings.recommendedFix: Make tombstoneMemory share deletion side effects: remove active_memory_projection rows, vector rows, FTS/BM25 documents, ancillary graph/entity rows, and invalidate caches, or uniformly add deleted_at IS NULL predicates to every active read path and ensure active projections exclude tombstones. Add regression tests for memory_delete and memory_bulk_delete with SPECKIT_SOFT_DELETE_TOMBSTONES=true across vector_search, fts5Bm25Search, trigger search, get_memories_by_folder, and memory_ingest/status-relevant stats.

findings.title: Async ingest reports rejected/error indexing results as successful files
findings.summary: The async ingest queue callback awaits indexSingleFile but discards the returned IndexResult. The job queue treats only thrown exceptions as per-file failures, increments filesProcessed afterward, and marks the job complete when failCount remains zero. Several indexing failure modes are returned as status='rejected' or status='error' rather than thrown, so operators can see a complete job with no errors while files were not indexed.

evidence.detail: initIngestJobQueue configures processFile to await indexSingleFile(...) and ignore its returned result.
indexSingleFile returns the IndexResult from indexMemoryFile.
indexMemoryFile/processPreparedMemory returns rejected/error IndexResult objects for quality, sufficiency, reconsolidation, and chunked supersession failures instead of throwing.
The worker catches exceptions, appends errors only in catch, increments processed, and marks the job complete when failCount is zero. Returned failed statuses never affect failCount.
memory_ingest_status surfaces only job.state, filesProcessed, and job.errors, so ignored IndexResult failures are invisible to callers.
The synchronous scan path already treats non-success statuses as failures and exposes rejection details, demonstrating the missing async ingest mapping.

findings.impact: Batch ingest can silently drop files rejected by quality/sufficiency/reconsolidation gates while presenting a complete, error-free job. Operators then have no retry signal and downstream memory coverage is incomplete. All-files-rejected jobs can still complete when none of the rejections throw.
findings.recommendedFix: Have the ingest processFile callback inspect IndexResult and throw or return a typed per-file outcome for any status outside the successful set used by memory_index_scan. Append rejectionCode/rejectionReason/error to job.errors, do not count rejected files as successful, and add tests where indexSingleFile returns rejected/error without throwing.
